chunk_summary: Guidelines for transitioning children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM) to rehabilitation focus on electrolyte safety, antibiotic protocols, and criteria-based discharge to prevent complications during refeeding.
learning_objectives: Understand the transition criteria from F-75 to F-100 or catch-up diets in SAM management.
Learn electrolyte replacement protocols, including potassium, magnesium, and phosphate correction during rehabilitation.
Recognize antibiotic regimens for uncomplicated and complicated SAM cases.
Identify discharge criteria and follow-up strategies for children recovering from SAM.
key_facts: Transition to rehabilitation should occur only after appetite improves, edema reduces, and weight gain is ≥5 g/kg/day for three consecutive days.
Potassium supplementation: 3–4 mEq/kg/day for at least two weeks; magnesium replacement includes initial IM dose and oral/enteral supplementation for two weeks.
Avoid added salt and diuretics during rehabilitation; check phosphate levels to prevent hypophosphatemia-related complications.
Routine antibiotics for uncomplicated SAM: oral amoxicillin 15 mg/kg per dose every 8 hours for five days.
Discharge criteria: clinically stable, feeding reliably, edema resolved, and upward weight trajectory.
reference_quote: Transition-phase feeding updates emphasize gradual progression from F-75 to F-100 or catch-up diets only when clinical stability and weight gain are evident. (Source: WHO guideline updates on SAM management)
